answer:
Definition

Architecting a Disaster Recovery (DR) strategy for 1M+ users involves designing systems that survive catastrophic failures — region outages, data corruption, total infrastructure loss — with defined Recovery Time Objective (RTO: how fast you recover) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO: how much data you can afford to lose). Strategies range from cold standby (hours RTO) to active-active multi-region (seconds RTO).

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled HR Leave Management System, Manoj would design a warm standby DR strategy: primary region serves all traffic with PostgreSQL streaming replication to a secondary region. RPO: 30 seconds (replication lag). RTO: 15 minutes (DNS failover + warm instance startup). The leave balance data is business-critical — even 1 minute of data loss could cause employees to overdraw their leave balance.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Automated failover detection and execution
interface HealthCheckResult {
  region: string;
  status: 'healthy' | 'degraded' | 'down';
  latencyMs: number;
  lastSuccessful: number;
}

class DisasterRecoveryOrchestrator {
  private primaryRegion = 'us-east-1';
  private drRegion = 'us-west-2';
  private failureThreshold = 3; // consecutive failures
  private failureCount = 0;

  async checkPrimaryHealth(): Promise {
    try {
      const start = Date.now();
      const res = await fetch(`https://${this.primaryRegion}.api.hrsystem.com/health`, {
        signal: AbortSignal.timeout(5000),
      });
      return {
        region: this.primaryRegion,
        status: res.ok ? 'healthy' : 'degraded',
        latencyMs: Date.now() - start,
        lastSuccessful: Date.now(),
      };
    } catch {
      return { region: this.primaryRegion, status: 'down', latencyMs: -1, lastSuccessful: 0 };
    }
  }

  async initiateFailover() {
    console.log('INITIATING DR FAILOVER');

    // Step 1: Promote PostgreSQL standby to primary
    await this.promotePGStandby();

    // Step 2: Scale up DR region instances
    await this.scaleUpDR();

    // Step 3: Update DNS to point to DR region
    await this.updateDNS(this.drRegion);

    // Step 4: Notify team
    await this.sendAlert('DR failover completed', {
      fromRegion: this.primaryRegion,
      toRegion: this.drRegion,
      timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
    });
  }

  private async promotePGStandby() {
    // pg_promote() call on standby server
    await fetch(`https://${this.drRegion}.internal/admin/pg-promote`, { method: 'POST' });
  }

  private async scaleUpDR() {
    // Scale from 1 warm instance to 3 active instances
    await fetch(`https://${this.drRegion}.internal/admin/scale`, {
      method: 'POST',
      body: JSON.stringify({ replicas: 3 }),
    });
  }

  private async updateDNS(targetRegion: string) {
    // Update Cloudflare DNS to point to DR region
    // TTL should be 60s so failover propagates quickly
  }
}

// Backup verification job (runs daily)
async function verifyBackups() {
  const latestBackup = await getLatestBackup();
  const ageHours = (Date.now() - latestBackup.timestamp) / 3600000;
  if (ageHours > 24) {
    await sendAlert('Backup is stale', { ageHours, threshold: 24 });
  }
  // Restore to test instance and verify row count
  const testDb = await restoreBackupToTestInstance(latestBackup);
  const rowCount = await testDb.query('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM leave_request');
  const prodCount = await prodDb.query('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM leave_request');
  if (Math.abs(rowCount - prodCount) > 100) {
    await sendAlert('Backup data drift detected', { testCount: rowCount, prodCount });
  }
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: During a planned DR drill, the team initiated failover to the DR region. The PostgreSQL standby was promoted successfully, but the Fastify application couldn't connect — it was still trying to connect to the primary region's database URL.

Investigation: Manoj found that the database connection string was hardcoded in the environment variables of the DR region's Fastify instances. When the standby was promoted, it became the new primary at a different internal IP, but the app config pointed to the old primary's hostname.

Fix: (1) Changed database config to use a DNS-based connection string (pg-primary.internal) that resolves to whichever PostgreSQL instance is currently primary. (2) Added a connection pool health check that retries with exponential backoff on connection failure. (3) Created a DR runbook that includes updating the DNS record as part of the failover sequence. (4) Added automated DR drills to run monthly.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
DR region never tested until real disasterMonthly automated DR drills with results reviewed
Hardcoded connection strings fail during failoverDNS-based service discovery; connection pool retries
Replication lag causes data loss on failoverMonitor replication lag; alert if > 30s; consider synchronous replication for critical data
DR region config drifts from primaryInfrastructure as Code (same templates); config diff check in CI

Validation Strategy

  • DR drill: full failover to DR region, serve production traffic for 1 hour, fail back — monthly
  • Replication lag: continuous monitoring, alert if lag exceeds 30 seconds
  • Backup restore: weekly restore of backup to test instance, verify data integrity
  • DNS failover: measure actual propagation time, verify < 5 minutes globally

Definition

Disaster Recovery performance bottlenecks surface during failover execution: slow DNS propagation delays traffic rerouting, database promotion takes longer than expected, cold-start instances need time to warm caches, and replication lag causes data inconsistency. Instrumenting and resolving these bottlenecks is critical to meeting RTO targets.

Concrete Use-Case

In the ContactsHub, Manoj deployed to Render with MongoDB Atlas as the database. During a simulated region failure, the failover took 45 minutes instead of the targeted 15 minutes. The bottlenecks: MongoDB Atlas automatic failover took 10 minutes (election + connection draining), Render's cold-start for new instances took 3 minutes each, and the Cloudinary CDN cache was region-specific, causing image 404s until re-cached.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Connection resilience for MongoDB failover
import { MongoClient } from 'mongodb';

const client = new MongoClient(process.env.MONGO_URL, {
  // DR-optimized connection settings
  serverSelectionTimeoutMS: 10000,  // Wait up to 10s for new primary
  heartbeatFrequencyMS: 5000,       // Check server status every 5s
  retryReads: true,
  retryWrites: true,
  readPreference: 'primaryPreferred', // Fall back to secondary during failover
  maxPoolSize: 20,
  minPoolSize: 5,                    // Keep connections warm
});

// Monitor connection events for DR awareness
client.on('serverHeartbeatFailed', (event) => {
  console.warn('MongoDB heartbeat failed:', event.connectionId);
  metrics.increment('mongodb.heartbeat.failed');
});

client.on('topologyDescriptionChanged', (event) => {
  const newPrimary = [...event.newDescription.servers.values()]
    .find(s => s.type === 'RSPrimary');
  if (newPrimary) {
    console.info('New MongoDB primary:', newPrimary.address);
    metrics.gauge('mongodb.primary.changed', 1);
  }
});

// Cache warming on startup
async function warmCache(redis) {
  console.log('Warming cache from database...');
  const hotKeys = await db.collection('cache_manifest').find({ priority: 'hot' }).toArray();
  for (const key of hotKeys) {
    const data = await db.collection(key.collection).findOne(key.query);
    if (data) await redis.setEx(key.cacheKey, key.ttl, JSON.stringify(data));
  }
  console.log(`Warmed ${hotKeys.length} cache entries`);
}

// Readiness probe that checks all dependencies
app.get('/health/ready', async (req, reply) => {
  const checks = await Promise.allSettled([
    client.db('admin').command({ ping: 1 }),
    redis.ping(),
    fetch(`${CLOUDINARY_URL}/health`).then(r => r.ok),
  ]);

  const allHealthy = checks.every(c => c.status === 'fulfilled');
  reply.code(allHealthy ? 200 : 503).send({
    status: allHealthy ? 'ready' : 'not_ready',
    checks: {
      mongodb: checks[0].status,
      redis: checks[1].status,
      cloudinary: checks[2].status,
    },
  });
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After a MongoDB Atlas region failover, ContactsHub was operational within 15 minutes, but users reported missing contact profile images. Contacts showed blank avatars for 2 hours after failover.

Investigation: Manoj found that Cloudinary image URLs contained region-specific transformation cache keys. When the CDN node in the primary region went down, the DR region's CDN had to re-process all image transformations from the original uploads. With 500K contact images, Cloudinary's on-the-fly processing queue was saturated.

Fix: (1) Pre-generated image transformations (thumbnail, medium, large) during upload, storing all variant URLs in MongoDB. (2) Used Cloudinary's eager transformations feature to create all sizes on upload. (3) Added a fallback UI that shows initials when avatar URL returns 404. (4) Configured Cloudinary's multi-CDN to serve from the nearest available POP, not region-specific.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
MongoDB election takes longer than expectedPre-provisioned secondary with higher priority; test election time monthly
Cold-start instances delay recoveryKeep warm standby instances running (1 replica minimum)
CDN cache invalidated during region failoverMulti-CDN configuration; eager image transformations on upload
DNS TTL too high, delays traffic reroutingSet DNS TTL to 60s for production domains

Validation Strategy

  • Monthly DR drill: full failover + failback with timing breakdown per step
  • Atlas failover test: trigger stepdown, measure time to new primary election
  • Image resilience: simulate CDN failure, verify fallback avatar (initials) displays
  • RTO tracking: maintain a dashboard showing actual vs. target RTO for each quarterly drill

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Blue-Green Deployments covers the strategy of maintaining two identical production environments (blue = current, green = new), routing traffic between them, validation procedures before cutover, failure modes (database schema mismatches, session loss, DNS propagation delays), and rollback plans (instant switch back to blue).

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System on Render, Manoj would implement blue-green deployments: the blue environment runs v2.3 (current production), while green runs v2.4 (new release with updated approval workflow). Both connect to the same PostgreSQL database. Before cutover: run Playwright E2E tests against green, verify leave approval flow works, check database migration compatibility. Cutover: update Render's service routing to point to green. Rollback: switch back to blue within 30 seconds.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Blue-green deployment automation script
interface DeployConfig {
  activeSlot: 'blue' | 'green';
  blueUrl: string;
  greenUrl: string;
  healthCheckPath: string;
  e2eTestCommand: string;
}

async function blueGreenDeploy(config: DeployConfig, newVersion: string) {
  const targetSlot = config.activeSlot === 'blue' ? 'green' : 'blue';
  const targetUrl = targetSlot === 'blue' ? config.blueUrl : config.greenUrl;

  console.log(`Deploying ${newVersion} to ${targetSlot}`);

  // Step 1: Deploy to inactive slot
  await deployToSlot(targetSlot, newVersion);

  // Step 2: Wait for health check
  let healthy = false;
  for (let i = 0; i < 30; i++) {
    try {
      const res = await fetch(`${targetUrl}${config.healthCheckPath}`);
      if (res.ok) { healthy = true; break; }
    } catch {}
    await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 2000));
  }
  if (!healthy) throw new Error(`${targetSlot} failed health check after 60s`);

  // Step 3: Run E2E tests against new slot
  const testResult = await runE2ETests(targetUrl);
  if (!testResult.passed) {
    console.error('E2E tests failed:', testResult.failures);
    throw new Error('E2E validation failed — aborting cutover');
  }

  // Step 4: Switch traffic
  console.log(`Switching traffic to ${targetSlot}`);
  await switchTraffic(targetSlot);

  // Step 5: Monitor for 5 minutes
  const monitorResult = await monitorErrorRate(targetUrl, 5 * 60 * 1000);
  if (monitorResult.errorRate > 0.01) {
    console.error('Error rate too high, rolling back');
    await switchTraffic(config.activeSlot); // Instant rollback
    throw new Error(`Rollback: error rate ${monitorResult.errorRate}`);
  }

  console.log(`Deployment of ${newVersion} to ${targetSlot} successful`);
  return { slot: targetSlot, version: newVersion };
}

// Database migration compatibility check
async function validateMigrationCompatibility(migration: string) {
  // Ensure migration is backward-compatible
  // Rule: only additive changes (new columns with defaults, new tables)
  // Never: drop columns, rename columns, change types
  const analysis = await analyzeMigration(migration);
  if (analysis.hasDestructiveChanges) {
    throw new Error(
      'Migration contains destructive changes. ' +
      'Use expand-contract pattern: add new column → migrate data → drop old column in next release.'
    );
  }
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After cutting over from Blue (v2.3) to Green (v2.4), some leave approval requests failed with "column 'approval_level' does not exist." The Green version's migration had renamed approval_step to approval_level, but during the 5-minute monitoring window, some in-flight requests from Blue (which was still draining) tried to read the renamed column.

Investigation: Manoj realized the migration was destructive — it renamed a column that Blue's code relied on. During the transition period, both Blue and Green were active, and Blue couldn't read the renamed column.

Fix: Adopted the expand-contract migration pattern: (1) v2.4 migration adds approval_level column (additive). (2) v2.4 code writes to both columns. (3) After Blue is fully drained, v2.5 migration drops approval_step. This ensures both versions can coexist with the same database schema.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Destructive migration breaks inactive slotExpand-contract pattern: only additive migrations during cutover
In-flight requests dropped during switchConnection draining: wait for active requests to complete before switching
Session state lost during cutoverStore sessions in Redis (shared between blue and green)
E2E tests pass but production traffic failsCanary period: send 5% traffic to green before full cutover

Validation Strategy

  • Migration compatibility: CI check that analyzes migration SQL, rejects destructive changes
  • Dual-version test: run blue and green simultaneously against same DB for 10 minutes
  • Rollback speed: measure time from rollback decision to traffic on blue — target < 30 seconds
  • Zero-downtime: monitor active connections during cutover, verify no dropped requests

Definition

Architecting Blue-Green Deployments for 1M+ users requires infrastructure that can maintain two identical production environments at scale, with instant traffic switching, shared persistent state (database, cache), automated validation pipelines, and cost optimization strategies for the idle environment.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled CRM Sales Dashboard serving 1M users, Manoj would implement blue-green with Render's service slots. Blue runs 10 instances of the Express.js API behind a load balancer. Green is deployed with the same 10-instance configuration. The shared MongoDB Atlas cluster handles both. Challenge: switching 1M active WebSocket connections (real-time deal updates) without dropping notifications during cutover.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// WebSocket-aware blue-green cutover
import { createAdapter } from '@socket.io/redis-adapter';
import { Server } from 'socket.io';
import { createClient } from 'redis';

// Both blue and green use the same Redis pub/sub
const pubClient = createClient({ url: process.env.REDIS_URL });
const subClient = pubClient.duplicate();

const io = new Server(server, {
  adapter: createAdapter(pubClient, subClient),
  // Graceful reconnection during cutover
  connectionStateRecovery: {
    maxDisconnectionDuration: 30 * 1000, // 30s buffer for cutover
    skipMiddlewares: true,
  },
});

// Graceful shutdown hook for blue during cutover
process.on('SIGTERM', async () => {
  console.log('Received SIGTERM — draining connections');

  // Stop accepting new connections
  server.close();

  // Give Socket.io clients 10s to reconnect to green
  io.emit('reconnect-hint', { reason: 'deployment', delayMs: 1000 });

  // Wait for in-flight HTTP requests to complete
  await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 10000));

  // Close remaining connections
  io.close();
  process.exit(0);
});

// Client-side reconnection handler (Angular/React)
// socket.on('reconnect-hint', () => {
//   // Exponential backoff reconnection
//   setTimeout(() => socket.connect(), Math.random() * 2000);
// });

// Weighted traffic routing for canary validation
interface TrafficConfig {
  blue: number;  // percentage
  green: number; // percentage
}

async function gradualCutover(steps: TrafficConfig[], intervalMs: number) {
  for (const step of steps) {
    await updateLoadBalancerWeights(step);
    console.log(`Traffic: Blue ${step.blue}%, Green ${step.green}%`);

    // Monitor error rate at each step
    const errorRate = await measureErrorRate(intervalMs);
    if (errorRate > 0.005) {
      console.error(`Error rate ${errorRate} too high at ${step.green}% green — rolling back`);
      await updateLoadBalancerWeights({ blue: 100, green: 0 });
      throw new Error('Canary failed');
    }
  }
}

// Example: 5% → 25% → 50% → 100% over 20 minutes
await gradualCutover([
  { blue: 95, green: 5 },
  { blue: 75, green: 25 },
  { blue: 50, green: 50 },
  { blue: 0, green: 100 },
], 5 * 60 * 1000);

Debugging Narrative

Situation: During a blue-green cutover of the CRM Dashboard, 30% of Socket.io connections dropped and didn't reconnect. Sales reps stopped receiving real-time deal update notifications for 5 minutes.

Investigation: Manoj found that the Socket.io Redis adapter was configured per-deployment, not shared. Blue's adapter published to socket.io#blue namespace, Green to socket.io#green. When clients reconnected to Green, they were in a different pub/sub namespace and missed events published during the transition.

Fix: (1) Configured both Blue and Green to use the same Redis adapter namespace (socket.io#prod). (2) Added a reconnect-hint event that tells clients to reconnect with a random delay (preventing reconnection storm). (3) Enabled Socket.io's connection state recovery — after reconnection, clients receive missed events from the buffer. (4) Added connection count monitoring: alert if active connections drop by more than 10% during cutover.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
WebSocket connections dropped during cutoverShared Redis adapter namespace + connection state recovery
Reconnection storm (1M clients reconnect simultaneously)Random delay in reconnect-hint (jitter) + connection backoff
Idle green environment is expensive at scaleRun at 20% capacity; scale to 100% before cutover
Database migrations not backward-compatibleExpand-contract pattern; CI check for destructive migrations

Validation Strategy

  • Connection continuity: monitor Socket.io connection count during cutover — < 5% drop allowed
  • Gradual cutover: route 5% → 25% → 50% → 100% with error rate check at each step
  • Cost audit: compare blue-green infra cost to rolling deployment — target < 30% overhead
  • Rollback test: at 50% traffic on green, trigger rollback — verify 100% back to blue within 30s

Definition

Horizontal Scaling performance bottlenecks occur when adding more instances doesn't proportionally increase throughput. Common culprits: shared-state contention (database connection limits, single Redis instance), sticky sessions preventing load distribution, uneven request routing, synchronization overhead between instances, and deployment infrastructure limits (load balancer capacity, DNS round-robin staleness).

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj scaled from 2 to 8 Fastify instances to handle month-end load. Throughput increased from 200 req/s to only 400 req/s (expected: 800 req/s). The bottleneck: all 8 instances shared a PostgreSQL connection pool of 20 max connections, and the leave balance update used a SELECT FOR UPDATE lock that serialized concurrent approvals for the same employee.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Before: Pessimistic locking serializes balance updates
async function debitLeaveBalance(employeeId: string, days: number) {
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    // SELECT FOR UPDATE — blocks all other transactions for this employee
    const balance = await tx.$queryRaw`
      SELECT * FROM leave_balance WHERE employee_id = ${employeeId} FOR UPDATE
    `;
    if (balance[0].remaining < days) throw new Error('Insufficient balance');
    await tx.leaveBalance.update({
      where: { employeeId },
      data: { remaining: { decrement: days } },
    });
  });
}

// After: Optimistic locking — no row locks
async function debitLeaveBalance(employeeId: string, days: number) {
  const maxRetries = 3;
  for (let attempt = 0; attempt < maxRetries; attempt++) {
    const balance = await prisma.leaveBalance.findUnique({ where: { employeeId } });
    if (balance.remaining < days) throw new Error('Insufficient balance');

    // Optimistic update: only succeeds if version hasn't changed
    const updated = await prisma.leaveBalance.updateMany({
      where: {
        employeeId,
        version: balance.version, // Optimistic lock check
      },
      data: {
        remaining: { decrement: days },
        version: { increment: 1 },
      },
    });

    if (updated.count === 1) return; // Success
    // Conflict — retry
    console.warn(`Optimistic lock conflict for ${employeeId}, attempt ${attempt + 1}`);
  }
  throw new Error('Failed after max retries — concurrent modification');
}

// PgBouncer configuration for connection pooling
// pgbouncer.ini:
// [databases]
// hrdb = host=pg-primary port=5432 dbname=hrleave
// [pgbouncer]
// pool_mode = transaction     # Release connection after each transaction
// max_client_conn = 1000      # Accept up to 1000 app connections
// default_pool_size = 50      # Only 50 actual PG connections
// reserve_pool_size = 10      # Extra connections for peak

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After scaling from 2 to 8 instances, the HR system's throughput only doubled instead of quadrupling. Response times under load were erratic — some requests completed in 50ms, others took 5 seconds.

Investigation: Manoj added Prometheus metrics for PostgreSQL connection pool usage. Found: each instance had max: 20 in the Prisma connection pool, but PostgreSQL's max_connections was 100. With 8 instances × 20 = 160 desired connections > 100 max, connection acquisition was failing and retrying. Additionally, the SELECT FOR UPDATE on leave balance meant two concurrent approvals for the same employee serialized at the database level.

Fix: (1) Introduced PgBouncer in transaction pooling mode — 8 instances × 20 connections connect to PgBouncer, which maintains only 50 real PostgreSQL connections. (2) Replaced pessimistic locking with optimistic locking (version column). (3) Removed session affinity from the load balancer — with stateless JWT auth, any instance can handle any request. (4) Added per-instance pool metrics. Result: 400 req/s → 900 req/s.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Connection pool exhaustion at scalePgBouncer for connection multiplexing; monitor pool usage per instance
Row-level locks prevent concurrent writesOptimistic locking with version column; retry on conflict
Sticky sessions create hot instancesStateless design (JWT + Redis sessions); round-robin routing
Uneven load distribution across instancesHealth-aware load balancer; monitor per-instance request rate

Validation Strategy

  • Linear scaling test: measure throughput at 2, 4, 8 instances — should be within 80% of linear
  • Connection pool test: simulate 8 instances × 20 connections; verify PgBouncer handles without rejections
  • Contention test: submit 100 concurrent leave approvals for same employee; verify all succeed or fail gracefully
  • Load balance test: send 10K requests, verify per-instance count varies by < 10%

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Horizontal Scaling covers scaling strategies (stateless application tier, database read replicas, sharding), trade-offs (complexity vs. throughput), failure modes (split-brain, uneven sharding, cascading instance failures), consistency challenges in distributed state, and rollback plans when scaling introduces bugs.

Concrete Use-Case

In ContactsHub, Manoj would scale the Fastify API horizontally to handle 1M users performing contact searches, CSV imports, and image uploads. The scaling strategy: stateless API instances behind Render's load balancer, MongoDB replica set with read preference secondaryPreferred for search queries, Redis for session storage, and Cloudinary for image processing (offloaded from the API tier entirely).

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Stateless API design for horizontal scaling
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import { createClient } from 'redis';

const app = Fastify();
const redis = createClient({ url: process.env.REDIS_URL });

// Stateless session: JWT + Redis for extended state
app.addHook('onRequest', async (req) => {
  const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
  if (!token) return;

  const payload = await verifyJWT(token);
  req.user = payload;

  // Extended user state from Redis (not in-process memory)
  const sessionData = await redis.get(`session:${payload.id}`);
  if (sessionData) req.session = JSON.parse(sessionData);
});

// Read scaling: route reads to secondary
import { MongoClient, ReadPreference } from 'mongodb';
const client = new MongoClient(process.env.MONGO_URL, {
  readPreference: ReadPreference.SECONDARY_PREFERRED,
});

// Writes go to primary (automatic)
app.post('/api/contacts', async (req) => {
  const contact = await db.collection('contacts').insertOne(req.body);
  return contact;
});

// Reads go to secondary (via readPreference)
app.get('/api/contacts/search', async (req) => {
  return db.collection('contacts')
    .find({ name: { $regex: req.query.q, $options: 'i' } })
    .toArray();
});

// Auto-scaling trigger
app.get('/metrics/scaling', async () => {
  const cpuUsage = process.cpuUsage();
  const memUsage = process.memoryUsage();
  const eventLoopLag = await measureEventLoopLag();

  return {
    cpu: cpuUsage,
    heapUsedMB: Math.round(memUsage.heapUsed / 1024 / 1024),
    eventLoopLagMs: eventLoopLag,
    shouldScale: eventLoopLag > 50 || memUsage.heapUsed / memUsage.heapTotal > 0.7,
  };
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: ContactsHub scaled from 2 to 6 instances, but search performance didn't improve. Some users even experienced slower results than before.

Investigation: Manoj checked MongoDB's connection metrics. All 6 instances were reading from the primary (default read preference). The primary was handling both reads and writes, and the 2 secondaries were idle. The application had readPreference: 'primary' hardcoded in the connection string from early development.

Fix: (1) Changed read preference to secondaryPreferred for search and list endpoints. (2) Created a dedicated read connection pool for search-heavy routes. (3) Added a search index on MongoDB secondary to handle full-text search without impacting the primary. (4) Monitored per-node operation count to verify reads distributed across replicas. Result: search p95 from 800ms → 200ms, and primary CPU dropped from 90% to 40%.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
All reads hit primary despite having replicasConfigure readPreference: secondaryPreferred for read-heavy routes
In-process state (sessions, cache) not shared across instancesRedis for all shared state; verify statelessness with chaos test
Cascading failure when instance count dropsNever run above 60% capacity; auto-scale at 50%
Scaling up doesn't help I/O-bound bottlenecksProfile first: if DB is the bottleneck, scale the DB (read replicas, caching)

Validation Strategy

  • Statelessness test: kill any instance mid-request, verify client retry succeeds on another instance
  • Linear scaling test: measure throughput at 2, 4, 8 instances — verify > 80% linear
  • Read distribution: monitor per-node ops/sec — reads should distribute across replicas
  • Cascading failure test: kill 50% of instances simultaneously, verify remaining instances survive the load spike
